# Abstract {-}

Integrating high-dimensional, multi-modal data requires synthesizing a shared representation that balances predictive capacity with mechanistic transparency. While deep learning methods excel at capturing non-linear manifold structure, they often obfuscate the relationship between input features and latent scores. We present a unified framework for Similarity-driven Multi-view Linear Representation (SiMLR) that bridges this gap by enforcing a "First-Layer Contract." This architectural constraint mandates a strictly linear encoding step, ensuring that every discovered latent component remains an exact, auditable projection of raw physical measurements.

Our framework utilizes deep non-linear decoders as an optimization scaffold to regularize the linear encoders. By partitioning the latent space into shared and private components, we explicitly sequester view-specific noise from the cross-modal consensus. These optimizations are implemented using Non-negative Stiefel approximating flow (NSA-Flow) to maintain weight orthogonality and Newton-Schulz iteration for latent stability and quadratic convergence. 

Through extensive synthetic benchmarks and real-world clinical datasets, we demonstrate that this framework frequently identifies linear subspaces that match or exceed the predictive accuracy of fully non-linear representations—a phenomenon we term "Transparency Parity." The primary takeaway is that the expressive power of deep networks can be effectively redirected to discover robust, low-variance linear bases. This allows practitioners to achieve state-of-the-art multi-modal alignment while retaining the exact, local audit trails required for precision medicine and scientific validation.
